proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored Steam and Metacritic text strongly and repeatedly identify Solateria as an 'action Metroidvania' with hand-drawn side-scrolling exploration, hidden areas unlocked through skills, shortcuts, free-order world exploration, and boss-focused parry combat. The core loop centers on traversing an interconnected world, acquiring new abilities/upgrades, uncovering secrets, and defeating powerful bosses, which cleanly fits metroidvania over RPG alternatives. The parry timing, difficulty tuning, and pattern-learning boss fights also support a soulslike-flavored secondary reading, but the dominant identity is exploration-platforming progression in a side-scrolling campaign.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: 2D metroidvania with a major emphasis on timing-based deflection/parry combat, boss duels, exploration, and ability-driven progression.
Benchmark combat-heavy metroidvania with interconnected exploration, tough bosses, map discovery, and gradual power unlocks.
Side-scrolling action metroidvania focused on demanding melee combat, traversal unlocks, boss encounters, and dark fallen-world storytelling.

staged_neighbors.candidate_title: Prince of Persia: The Lost Crown
staged_neighbors.requested_title: Prince of Persia: The Lost Crown
staged_neighbors.why_similar: Fast, polished metroidvania centered on precision combat, parries, mobility upgrades, and a guided-but-open world structure.

staged_neighbors.candidate_title: ENDER LILIES: Quietus of the Knights
staged_neighbors.requested_title: Ender Lilies: Quietus of the Knights
staged_neighbors.why_similar: Atmospheric combat-focused metroidvania with boss-gated progression, build customization, and a ruined world explored through nonlinear upgrades.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently present Collector's Cove as a cozy, non-combat farming adventure centered on cultivating a floating farm, fishing, crafting, personalizing a ship-home, exploring islands, collecting entries for a compendium, and bonding with an animal companion. Multiple sources repeat the same core loop and explicitly state 'There are no enemies in this world,' which strongly supports a farming/life-sim identity over action or survival archetypes. The game appears single-player, casual, long-form, and exploration-driven, with progression through farm growth, collection, personalization, and companion relationship building.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Cozy farming and crafting loop centered on sailing between islands, gathering resources, growing crops, and light quest-driven exploration.
Gentle fishing-heavy progression with collection goals, crafting, and tranquil exploration rather than combat pressure.
Recognizable cozy farming template with crop cultivation, fishing, collecting, crafting, and steady completion-driven progression.
Relaxed collecting, fishing, crafting, customization, and low-stakes daily progression that matches the cozy, non-combat appeal.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored store and aggregator descriptions consistently identify WWE 2K26 as a wrestling simulation/sports title centered on matches, roster-based competition, and multiple structured modes. Evidence repeatedly mentions new match types, PvP and co-op support, Showcase/MyRISE single-player campaigns, Universe/MyGM management layers, and MyFACTION team competition. Steam labels include Simulation and Sports, while the copy emphasizes winning matches, realistic wrestling presentation, and replayable seasonal/live-service-adjacent content. Although wrestling is not a dedicated archetype in the allowed list, sports_sim is the best fit for its core gameplay identity.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Immediate predecessor with the same sim-style wrestling engine, WWE match structure, creation suite, and mode stack including Showcase/Universe/MyGM-style play.
Another near-identical modern WWE 2K entry centered on realistic pro wrestling matches, licensed roster play, Showcase content, and deep sandbox/creation modes.
Shares the same core WWE 2K gameplay loop of simulation-flavored ring action, career/story content, faction collecting, and booking/management side modes.
Modern foundation for the current WWE 2K formula, mixing arcade-accessible controls with simulation presentation, licensed wrestlers, and extensive custom creation.
Closest major non-WWE wrestling comp: a roster-based pro wrestling game focused on match cards, multiplayer bouts, wrestler movesets, and ring-based combat progression.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently identify the game as a third-person cinematic action-adventure centered on a linear heroic rescue quest through semi-open historical-medieval Italy. Core play is melee sword combat with two combat stances, boss-oriented encounters, authored story progression, exploration of handcrafted locations, and light collection/progression via skill books and weapon customization. This strongly fits cinematic_action_adventure; character-action and soulslike traits are present but less central because the material emphasizes cinematic narrative journey and setpiece adventure over pure combo mastery or explicitly punishing soulslike structure.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Very close fit on third-person cinematic action-adventure structure, authored journey, melee-heavy combat, progression unlocks, collectibles, and major boss encounters; more overtly mythic and polished, but gameplay identity is highly aligned.

Narrative-driven third-person campaign with melee-centric combat, ability progression, exploration detours, traversal gating, and boss fights. Slightly more soulslike/metroidvania in structure, but still a very good gameplay neighbor.

Strong authored cinematic melee action with historical flavor and spectacle, but it is more corridor-driven and encounter-setpiece focused, with less of the anchor's semi-open exploration and progression texture.
Third-person melee-focused journey with boss-centric progression, authored regions, exploration detours, and ability/gear growth.
Cinematic single-player action-adventure with semi-open hubs, collectible-driven exploration, skill unlocks, and frequent boss fights.

current_live_neighbors.title: Indiana Jones and the Great Circle

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently define Solasta II as a 'Turn-Based Tactical RPG' built around a faithful tabletop-style ruleset, with the player creating and guiding a party of 4 adventurers through a quest-driven campaign. The evidence strongly supports party control, tactical party-based combat, quest/exploration progression, and choice-driven CRPG structure. The text also indicates freer roaming across Neokos rather than purely linear mission flow, which fits a semi-open campaign CRPG better than a pure tactics game. Turn-based tactics is a strong secondary identity, but the party creation, roleplay framing, questing, and choice emphasis make party_based_crpg the better primary archetype.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Direct predecessor: same studio, party-of-4 tactical RPG structure, turn-based D&D-style combat, and heavy emphasis on rules-faithful spell and ability use.
Party-based CRPG with turn-based encounters, systemic D&D-derived combat, environmental tactics, dialogue choices, and quest-led exploration.
Companion-driven fantasy CRPG focused on party building, character progression, tactical battles, and a long-form choice-heavy campaign.
Squad-based turn-based RPG emphasizing encounter tactics, party synergies, exploration, and flexible quest progression across a campaign.
Modern party CRPG with turn-based tactical combat, extensive character builds, companion management, and branching quest-driven progression.

current_live_neighbors.title: Labyrinth of Galleria: The Moon Society

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored Steam and Metacritic text consistently describe a first-person 4-player co-op shooter focused on surviving and clearing massive undead/mutant hordes, reviving teammates, using classes/abilities, and traversing semi-open maps with drivable vehicles. The core loop reads much closer to cooperative horror-action than to military FPS, hero shooter, or arena FPS. Tone is pulpy rather than pure horror, but the infected/undead threat, survival framing, and squad-based horde combat strongly support co_op_horror as the closest taxonomy fit.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: The clearest comp: 4-player co-op missions, enormous zombie swarms via Saber’s swarm tech, class-based loadouts, and objective-driven horde shooting.
Modern 4-player zombie co-op shooter with team synergy, specials mixed into large hordes, and run-based progression/loadout building.
Wave-based co-op FPS centered on class perks, heavy gunplay, and mowing down dense monster hordes with a coordinated team.
Co-op undead shooting across campaign stages with set-piece swarm fights, squad support, and progression that emphasizes fighting through hordes together.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Store text consistently describes a cozy single-player shop-life loop: inherit and run a family repair shop, restore customer items, grow berries for paint, decorate the shop, bake cookies, clean and maintain the workspace, and interact with neighbors. The core identity is non-combat, low-pressure domestic/occupational simulation with customization and social-town flavor, which fits life_sim best. Repairing objects 'each with its own puzzle' adds a light hidden-object/puzzle-adjacent layer, while selling items and maintaining the store gives a management secondary aspect, but the evidence most strongly supports a cozy life-sim structure rather than a pure tycoon or puzzle game.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: The clearest match: cozy single-player restoration of sentimental household objects through hands-on repair puzzles, with townsfolk stories closely mirroring the anchor's core loop.
Strong repair-for-clients fit with wholesome puzzle/adventure elements and low-pressure tone, though it is more traversal- and exploration-driven than shop-life focused.

Good overlap in cozy artisan shopkeeping, serving townsfolk, crafting outputs, and profit/customization loops, but potion-brewing systems are farther from object restoration puzzles and domestic life sim play.
Matches the cute life-sim structure, shopkeeping, decorating, chores, and town interaction, but its broader farming/exploration/crafting sandbox is less centered on repair and item-restoration tasks.
Antique restoration is the main loop, with hands-on repair tasks, cleaning, refurbishing, and reselling in a relaxed solo sim structure.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: The stored description clearly identifies the game as a 'brainteasing precision platformer' built around single-screen challenges across 50 unique levels. Core play is running and leaping through increasingly complex stages, with explicit platforming plus puzzle gating via freezing time, shifting perspectives, pushing crates, activating switches, and using the cat companion's body as an improvised platform. This strongly supports a precision_platformer classification with level-based, side-scrolling 2D, non-combat gameplay.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Very close on 2D precision-platforming, compact room challenges, puzzle-inflected traversal, and retry-heavy execution. Less aligned on structure because VVVVVV is more interconnected/open than the anchor’s explicitly level-based single-screen stages.

Strong match for single-screen puzzle structure and body-based spatial problem solving, which echoes the cat-as-platform idea. Weaker as a direct comp because it is primarily a gridlike physics puzzle game rather than a run-and-jump precision platformer.
Tight 2D level-based precision platforming with short stages, instant retries, and execution-focused obstacle runs.
Room-based precision platforming built around discrete single-screen challenges, momentum control, and high difficulty.
Hard 2D platformer with compact stages, exacting jumps, and dense hazard-driven challenge progression.

title: Fatal Frame II: Crimson Butterfly Remake

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored Steam and Metacritic text consistently describe a Japanese-style horror adventure centered on exploring an abandoned haunted village, uncovering its secrets, and repelling ghosts with the Camera Obscura. The copy emphasizes horror atmosphere, darkness, exploration, escape, and updated combat elements rather than pure action, which strongly fits survival_horror. Single-player campaign structure is directly supported, and the village mystery plus ghost encounters suggest a guided exploratory survival-horror loop.

staged_neighbors.candidate_title: Fatal Frame: Maiden of the Black Water
staged_neighbors.requested_title: Fatal Frame: Maiden of Black Water
staged_neighbors.why_similar: Another Fatal Frame entry built around photographing ghosts, survival-horror exploration, and story-driven supernatural investigation.

staged_neighbors.candidate_title: Fatal Frame: Mask of the Lunar Eclipse
staged_neighbors.requested_title: Fatal Frame: Mask of the Lunar Eclipse
staged_neighbors.why_similar: Closely matches the remake’s loop of eerie exploration, fixed-purpose item use, and camera-based spirit encounters.
Also revolves around navigating haunted environments and using a camera to expose and repel supernatural enemies.

missing_must_include_titles: Fatal Frame: Maiden of Black Water
Fatal Frame: Mask of the Lunar Eclipse

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored evidence consistently identifies Galactic Vault as a single-player first-person shooter roguelite with fast-paced combat, permanent arsenal expansion, buildcraft via weapon attachments and upgrade combinations, boss encounters, and room-based infiltration through vaults. However, the available Similar Games Taxonomy V2 archetype set does not contain a clear FPS roguelite / boomer-shooter-roguelite bucket. The strongest candidate, arena_fps, is not well supported because the game is not described as match-based or arena-only and instead emphasizes single-player roguelite progression. Immersive_sim_action is also not sufficiently supported because the text does not establish systemic problem solving, multiple infiltration approaches, or immersive-sim interaction depth. Rejecting due to taxonomy fit gap rather than lack of gameplay evidence.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Fast first-person roguelite runs with high mobility, room-to-room combat, build synergies, bosses, and meta progression.
Cyberpunk FPS roguelite built around aggressive movement, short combat arenas, powerful build combinations, and repeat runs.
Run-based FPS with randomized upgrades, weapon-focused buildcraft, bosses, and persistent progression across attempts.
Single-player FPS roguelite emphasizing quick arena shooting, procedurally structured runs, perk builds, and repeatable progression.
Fast-paced first-person roguelite with room-clearing combat, varied weapons, upgrade choices, and boss encounters.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored store descriptions consistently and repeatedly identify Haunted Lands as a retro-inspired action platformer. The evidence strongly supports a side-scrolling, level-based single-player campaign built around platform traversal and dominant combat: players slay hordes of cultists and monsters, use six distinct characters with different close-range and ranged playstyles, acquire artifacts that alter abilities, uncover secrets, and fight challenging bosses. Horror setting and bleak/grotesque tone are explicit, but the core gameplay identity is much more action-platformer than horror-specialist archetypes. The prior computed candidate pointing to sports_sim is clearly spurious and contradicted by all textual evidence.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Retro 2D action platforming built around multiple distinct playable characters, challenging stages, secrets, and boss-focused progression.
Hardcore retro action platformer with gothic horror enemies, exploration, secrets, upgrades, and demanding combat encounters.
Brutal side-scrolling action platformer emphasizing heavy combat, grotesque enemies, set-piece bosses, and a bloody old-school feel.
Dark fantasy 2D action platformer with punishing combat, horror aesthetics, and a strong focus on clearing hostile stages and bosses.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored evidence clearly establishes a cooperative multiplayer add-on with two-player story missions, four-player survival matches, class selection, map-based territory control, and wave-based enemy defense in a supernatural setting. However, the available text does not firmly establish a supported taxonomy archetype from the allowed list. It does not provide reliable evidence for perspective, traversal, long-term progression, or roguelite structure, and the DLC context does not include a parent game to inherit core gameplay identity from. The mode appears closer to a co-op wave/survival action experience, but that exact archetype is not available in the taxonomy, so assigning a nearest-fit archetype would be too speculative.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Near-direct template: supernatural class-based co-op with 2-player missions, 4-player survival, matchmaking, and wave-based PvE combat.
Essential base-game pairing, and Legends likely inherits core combat feel and traversal, though the base game is primarily a solo open-world action experience.
Strong samurai/yokai melee action and online co-op overlap, but it is a tougher mission-based soulslike ARPG with a different progression and challenge model.
Very relevant for 4-player class-based co-op, horde pressure, and replayable PvE runs, but first-person structure and combat identity differ sharply.

staged_neighbors.candidate_title: Stranger of Paradise: Final Fantasy Origin
staged_neighbors.requested_title: Stranger of Paradise: Final Fantasy Origin
staged_neighbors.why_similar: Mission-based co-op PvE with melee-heavy combat, distinct job classes, and supernatural enemy encounters.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored store descriptions consistently identify the core loop as a 'chaotic Roguelite Twin-Stick Shooter with a Base Defense Twist' playable with up to 4 players. The text describes repeated runs with time reversal, retaining experience/resources between attempts, which strongly supports roguelite run structure plus metaprogression. Moment-to-moment play centers on exploring a planet, gathering resources, fighting monsters in arena encounters and waves, then returning to defend the crashed ship using crafted ramparts, turrets, and traps. Combat is dominant, fast, and top-down/twin-stick in identity, with multiple class/loadout styles spanning melee, explosives, flamethrowers, turrets, missiles, dashes, and ranged attacks. Co-op is explicitly emphasized in Steam categories and main copy, making co_op_action_roguelite the best fit. The base-defense/crafting layer is meaningful but additive rather than enough to displace the action-roguelite identity.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Very close fit: co-op roguelite action with top-down/tactical shooting, procedural runs, turret placement, and constant protection of a fragile core objective under wave pressure.
Strong structural match in co-op explore-gather-return-defend flow, escalating assaults, crafting, and central-base protection, though it leans more survival ARPG than twin-stick shooter.
Good comp for co-op hero combat plus building defenses against waves, but it is more lane/tower-defense mission-based and less roguelite run/exploration-driven than the anchor.

Shares top-down action, resource collection, construction, and surviving major enemy waves around a growing base, but its factory/base-building focus and less run-based roguelite structure make it a looser fit.
Co-op action roguelite where each run revolves around building defenses and personally fighting through wave-based holds.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently identify Mr. Sleepy Man as a 3D platformer with an expressive moveset ('jump, glide, and dive'), interconnected levels, heavy exploration, secrets, collectibles, and hundreds of objectives. The core loop centers on platform navigation, roaming semi-open handcrafted spaces, interacting with objects/NPCs, and completing collectathon-style objectives rather than combat. The tone is overtly comedic and whimsical, matching a 3D collectathon identity more strongly than a metroidvania. Action-platformer is a secondary fit because of the movement-focused traversal and chaos-oriented play, but collectible/exploration structure is the clearer primary gameplay identity.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: A premier 3D collectathon comp: movement-centric traversal, semi-open kingdoms, constant secret/objective hunting, and dense interactive spaces closely match the anchor's core loop.

Very close fit on expressive 3D platforming, collectible-driven progression, exploration, and playful stage design; it has more combat than the anchor, but the collectathon identity is still highly aligned.

staged_neighbors.candidate_title: SpongeBob SquarePants: Battle For Bikini Bottom - Rehydrated
staged_neighbors.requested_title: SpongeBob SquarePants: Battle for Bikini Bottom - Rehydrated
staged_neighbors.why_similar: Hub-linked 3D platforming, collectibles, NPC tasks, and exploration-heavy levels fit well, though it is more combat-forward and less sandbox-chaos driven than the anchor.
Strong overlap in environmental mischief, reactive sandbox play, and traversal-led objectives, but it is fundamentally an open-world chaos sim rather than a 3D collectathon platformer.
Third-person comedic platforming with hub exploration, collectible hunting, expressive movement, and character-driven NPC spaces over combat-heavy play.

current_live_neighbors.title: Bō: Path of the Teal Lotus

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently identify Crabmeat as a short first-person, mouse-only, point-and-click survival horror experience about operating a crabbing vessel, collecting quota within 7 days, navigating Antarctic waters, maintaining traps/ship functions, and defending against underwater threats. That is enough to infer a horror adventure with light survival/vehicle-management elements, but it does not cleanly map to any single allowed archetype. The current computed hidden_object_puzzle candidate appears overfit to point-and-click/puzzle signals and is not well supported by the actual described loop, while survival_horror/psychological_horror are also not securely established because the evidence does not clearly describe the genre-defining combat/resource/scare structure expected for those archetypes. Strong evidence exists for a hybrid first-person point-and-click horror adventure, but not for a confident placement into the available taxonomy archetypes.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Closest overall loop match: small-boat tasking, hostile sea navigation, quota/quest progression, upgrades, and horror pressure from what emerges in the water, even if it is broader and more systems-driven than Crabmeat.

Very strong fit on short-form first-person craft operation, claustrophobic horror, limited interface-driven interaction, and dread-led progression; less similar on fishing/work loop and point-and-click adventure structure.

Shares first-person shipboard exploration, authored mystery progression, and deliberate interaction/puzzle solving, but it is a pure deduction mystery with no survival-work loop or active vessel management.

Good match for experimental horror adventure, mouse/interface-driven play, and puzzle gating over combat, but its anthology structure and varied input styles are less aligned with Crabmeat's nautical vehicle-operation loop.
Short-form horror adventure with pure point-and-click play, authored progression, environmental puzzle solving, and constant dread.

current_live_neighbors.title: The Order of the Thorne - The King's Challenge
Harold Rabbit - Finder of Lost Things

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently identify Granblue Fantasy as a classic JRPG with a deep turn-based combat system, elemental strategy, classes, party-building, character/weapon progression, and a main quest plus character story episodes. The text strongly supports a story-driven, party-command RPG identity over any other candidate. Real-time co-op raids are present, but they read as an additive mode layered onto the core authored campaign JRPG structure rather than replacing it. The fighting/match-based signals in the prior computation appear to be false positives from words like raids, combos, and co-op rather than evidence of a traditional fighter.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Turn-based party RPG centered on team composition, character builds, skill synergies, and ongoing story content.

staged_neighbors.candidate_title: Another Eden: The Cat Beyond Time and Space
staged_neighbors.requested_title: Another Eden: The Cat Beyond Time and Space
staged_neighbors.why_similar: Single-player-first JRPG with authored story progression, party management, and classic turn-based combat.

current_live_neighbors.title: Monster Hunter Stories 2: Wings of Ruin

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored evidence consistently describes a single-player, turn-based, party-commanded dungeon-crawling roguelike RPG with permadeath, procedural generation, recruitable characters, and skill learning. However, the available taxonomy archetypes do not cleanly fit this identity. The evidence does not establish key archetype-defining traits needed for turn_based_tactics or tactical_rpg, such as tactical-overhead presentation, explicit battlefield structure, or clearer tactical-RPG conventions; nor does it support jrpg_story_rpg, party_based_crpg, or card_battler. Because the gameplay is real but sits between supported archetypes and the strongest candidate mapping would be too inferential, classification should be rejected rather than forced.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Very close match on party-based turn-based expeditions, recruit/train roster loop, attrition, permadeath pressure, and grim dungeon progression.
Party-led turn-based roguelike dungeon crawling with changing maps, harsh survival pressure, formation tactics, and one-life consequences is an excellent fit.
Strong overlap in turn-based roguelike dungeon crawling, procedural labyrinth structure, permadeath options, and deep skill/job progression, though it is less explicitly party-centric.
Excellent party dungeon-crawling and turn-based combat comp, but its core structure is more classic authored DRPG than roguelike run-based procedural attrition.
Turn-based party roguelite runs with permadeath-style attrition, dungeon progression, and roster-building between expeditions.

removed_candidates: Shiren the Wanderer: The Tower of Fortune and the Dice of Fate

title: Age of Wonders 4: Rise from Ruin

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Accepted using DLC inheritance from the parent game because the DLC description is additive only: it adds a new culture, form, tomes, wildlife, terrain, quests, and realm traits, and does not describe a different core mode or genre. Parent-game evidence explicitly describes 'Age of Wonders’ signature blend of 4X strategy and turn-based tactical combat,' empire expansion over turns, realm exploration, customizable factions, city/empire growth, tactical battles, and replayable realm generation. The DLC copy reinforces the same identity with battlefield mobility, territory seizure, harvesting ancient wonders, new world-map content, epic quests, and new tomes. This strongly supports 4x_strategy as primary, with turn-based tactical battles as a secondary gameplay layer.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: The parent game; this DLC directly inherits its fantasy 4X strategy loop, empire customization, and turn-based tactical combat.
Another Triumph 4X that pairs strategic empire management with separate turn-based tactical battles and faction customization.
Very close series match with fantasy realm expansion, hero-led armies, city building, and tactical turn-based encounters.
Fantasy 4X focused on expanding cities, researching faction-specific powers, and fighting tactical turn-based battles on the map.
Turn-based fantasy strategy centered on roaming armies, magical progression, realm control, and tactical combat in a campaign structure.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored evidence is limited to a single short Metacritic-style description describing a simple comedic action game where the player controls a fly, dodges swats, and annoys students in a classroom. While this suggests arcade-style evasive action with timing-heavy movement, the evidence does not strongly map to any allowed Similar Games Taxonomy V2 archetype. It does not clearly establish platforming, shooter structure, racing, horror, RPG systems, puzzle structure, tactics, sim management, or another supported core gameplay identity. Because the gameplay appears to fall outside the available archetype set and the source base is sparse, rejection is more reliable than forcing an imprecise fit.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Strongest overall thematic-mechanical match: short-form comedic chaos, simple controls, level-based annoyance objectives, and escalating ways to ruin NPCs' day. Broader and more ability-driven than the anchor, but still a close neighbor.

Very good fit on mischievous harassment of humans and movement-driven prank play. Less aligned on immediate arcade survival, since it is more stealth-sandbox and objective puzzling than pure dodge-and-annoy action.

Shares slapstick disruption, score-chasing chaos, and funny reactions in ordinary spaces, but it is much more physics-sandbox destruction focused and less about precise evasion or building irritation meters.
Play as an annoying insect harassing humans while dodging swats in a tightly matched evasive-action setup.
Lighthearted destructive nuisance gameplay focused on being a small animal causing immediate indoor chaos.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored evidence strongly supports a cozy single-player non-combat puzzle/simulation game centered on organizing, rotating, and fitting items into boxes under space constraints, with light story and workspace customization. However, that gameplay identity does not cleanly map to any allowed Similar Games Taxonomy V2 archetype. The computed near-match of hidden_object_puzzle is not well supported by the text, which describes spatial packing/optimization rather than hidden object search, point-and-click investigation, or mystery solving. Because the available evidence is strong but indicates an out-of-taxonomy organization/packing puzzle rather than an existing archetype, rejection is more appropriate than forcing an inaccurate fit.

staged_neighbors.why_similar: Near-perfect comp: calm item placement, unpacking/packing identity, constrained spatial fitting, environmental storytelling, and a cozy single-player non-combat loop.
Very good fit on tidy organization puzzling, casual structure, and satisfying arrangement, though it is more abstract observational sorting than box-packing optimization.
Strong organization/space-management puzzle overlap with warehouse framing and progression, but its retrieval/memory pressure and more systemic layout play make it less close than Unpacking.
Strong on rotating and fitting oddly shaped items into tight spaces, but the Resident Evil-style inventory logic and resource constraints are a more gamified, less cozy version of the loop.
Tight spatial-fitting puzzles built around arranging shaped items neatly within confined containers, with a calm cozy presentation.
